name: Apache Kafka via Confluent Platform
description: Self-managed Apache Kafka deployed with Confluent Platform for event streaming, durable logs, and integration pipelines.

ref_arch.version: Confluent Platform current self-managed deployment guidance

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 50 MB/s ingress with short retention windows

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 50 to 150 MB/s ingress or larger retained topic footprints

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 150 to 400 MB/s ingress with longer retention and more connectors

notes: Broker, controller, Connect, and Control Center roles follow Confluent's published self-managed deployment guidance and are rounded to CloudEstimate's VM shape set.
The base tiers already assume quorum and replication on the Kafka side, so HA overhead is baked into the baseline topology.
disclaimers: Based on Confluent Platform self-managed deployment guidance and translated into VM-based planning tiers.
Estimate excludes producer and consumer application hosts, ksqlDB, Flink services, tiered storage, network transfer, and Confluent licensing.

name: CockroachDB Self-Hosted
description: Self-hosted CockroachDB for resilient SQL workloads that need distributed transactions and scale-out storage.

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 1 TB replicated OLTP data on a three-node cluster

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 1 to 5 TB replicated OLTP data on a five-node cluster

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 5 to 20 TB replicated data or higher-concurrency regional SQL workloads

notes: CockroachDB production guidance starts at a three-node quorum, so redundancy is already reflected in the base topology.
Node sizes align with the product's published CPU, memory, and SSD guidance for production clusters and are normalized to the nearest CloudEstimate VM shape.
disclaimers: Based on CockroachDB self-hosted production recommendations and expressed as VM-based planning tiers.
Estimate excludes load balancers, backups, changefeeds, geo-partitioned layouts, and network transfer between regions or zones.

name: Confluence Data Center
description: Self-managed Confluence Data Center for enterprise documentation, knowledge bases, and collaborative editing.

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 500,000 to 2.5 million content items or 70,000 to 350,000 HTTP calls per hour

l.label: Large
l.range_description: 2.5 million to 10 million content items or 350,000 to 700,000 HTTP calls per hour

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 10 million to 25 million content items or 700,000 to 1,000,000 HTTP calls per hour

notes: Medium, Large, and XLarge tiers map to Atlassian's published Confluence Data Center benchmark recommendations, with Synchrony added from Atlassian's sample enterprise deployment topology.
The Large tier uses the low-cost recommendation, while the XLarge tier uses the stability recommendation to preserve cluster fault tolerance.
disclaimers: Estimate excludes shared-home EFS/NFS storage, collaborative editing proxy details, Marketplace app overhead, and managed database HA costs not captured in the benchmark summary.
The HA toggle models load balancer overhead only; clustered Confluence application and Synchrony nodes are already represented in the base deployment.

name: Consul
description: Self-managed Consul for service discovery, service mesh control, and network configuration across distributed workloads.

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 500 registered services on a three-server control plane

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 500 to 5,000 registered services with ingress and mesh gateways

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 5,000 to 25,000 registered services with larger service-mesh control needs

notes: Consul sizing is focused on the control plane and common gateway roles; the client agents embedded on application hosts are not modelled as separate infrastructure here.
Odd-numbered server counts follow HashiCorp's quorum guidance for production control planes.
disclaimers: Based on Consul production architecture guidance and translated into VM-based planning tiers.
Estimate excludes application workload nodes, external L7 gateways, network transfer, and any HCP-managed alternatives.

name: Couchbase Server Enterprise
description: Self-managed Couchbase Server Enterprise for distributed JSON data, caching, and full-text search workloads.

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 50 GB active data on a compact multi-service cluster

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 50 to 250 GB active data with separated data, query, and index services

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 250 GB to 1 TB active data on a larger dedicated-services cluster

notes: Data node memory follows Couchbase's published data-service sizing ratio, while query and index roles follow the vendor's CPU and RAM guidance per service.
The larger tiers assume bucket replication is already built into the base cluster, so the HA toggle does not add extra nodes.
disclaimers: Based on Couchbase Server sizing guidance and translated into VM-oriented planning tiers for multi-cloud comparison.
Estimate excludes XDCR, backup service overhead, Analytics or Eventing nodes, and storage growth from replicas or compaction beyond the local disks shown.

name: EDB Postgres Advanced Server
description: Self-managed EDB Postgres Advanced Server for enterprise PostgreSQL workloads with optional primary-standby HA.

ref_arch.version: EPAS 18 requirements with current HA deployment guidance

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 250 GB database size for light OLTP

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 250 GB to 1 TB database size for mixed read and write OLTP

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 1 to 5 TB database size or heavier mixed OLTP and reporting

notes: The documented installation minimums for EPAS are not meaningful production sizes, so these tiers are derived from EDB's primary-standby HA guidance and normalized to cloud VM shapes.
HA mode adds standbys, failover management, and connection-entry infrastructure rather than assuming those costs are present in the non-HA baseline.
disclaimers: Based on EDB Postgres Advanced Server requirements and public high-availability guidance, translated into VM-based planning tiers.
Estimate excludes backup storage, WAL archiving, tuning-specific IOPS requirements, logical replication, and any separate management tooling.

name: Elasticsearch OSS
description: Legacy-style self-managed Elasticsearch baseline for community and OSS estates running search and log analytics clusters.

ref_arch.version: Current self-managed guidance; OSS distribution lineage froze at 7.10

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 100 GB hot index data on a single node

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 100 GB to 1 TB hot data on a dedicated master cluster

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 1 to 5 TB hot data on a scale-out search cluster

notes: The OSS distribution is a legacy concept after the 7.10 fork, so this entry should be read as a community-style self-managed Elasticsearch planning baseline.
Hot-tier node sizes follow Elasticsearch self-managed heap and storage guidance, normalized to the VM shapes available in CloudEstimate.
disclaimers: Based on Elastic's public self-managed operational guidance and translated into a legacy OSS-style VM baseline.
Estimate excludes snapshots, warm or frozen tiers, security features tied to commercial distributions, machine learning nodes, and network transfer.

name: GitLab Self-Managed
description: Self-managed GitLab for source control, CI/CD, and DevSecOps workflows.
notes: Storage becomes the primary cost driver as repository and artifact data scales.
Estimate excludes GitLab licensing, support, network egress, compliance, and backup storage.
disclaimers: Based on GitLab's published reference architectures and simplified to a VM-based baseline.
Production deployments should be verified against workload-specific RPS, monorepos, and CI patterns.

name: InfluxDB Enterprise
description: Self-managed InfluxDB Enterprise for high-ingest time-series monitoring, telemetry, and operational analytics.

ref_arch.version: InfluxDB Enterprise v1 hardware sizing guidelines

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 100,000 writes per second and under 1 million series

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 100,000 to 400,000 writes per second and 1 to 5 million series

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 400,000 to 750,000 plus writes per second and 5 to 10 million plus series

notes: Meta nodes stay small and odd-numbered, while data nodes scale out in even counts to reflect the vendor's own cluster guidance.
Write-rate and series-cardinality bands are taken directly from InfluxDB Enterprise sizing guidance and normalized to CloudEstimate's billable VM shapes.
disclaimers: Based on InfluxDB Enterprise hardware sizing guidance and translated into VM-based planning tiers.
Estimate excludes Kapacitor, query relays, backup infrastructure, object storage, and network transfer between zones or regions.

name: JFrog Artifactory Self-Hosted
description: Self-hosted JFrog Artifactory for binary repositories, release promotion, and software supply chain distribution.
notes: Node counts are derived from JFrog Artifactory sizing templates, with storage taken from the companion storage specifications page.
Local SSD in this estimate reflects node cache and temp storage only; external filestore object storage is not fully modelled.
disclaimers: Based on JFrog self-managed sizing guidance and translated from the published cloud-native reference templates into VM-style capacity estimates.
Estimate excludes Artifactory licensing, Xray, distribution edge nodes, and artifact blob storage beyond the local node disks shown.

name: Jira Data Center
description: Self-managed Jira Data Center for enterprise issue tracking and service management workloads.

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: above the Large Jira profile thresholds

notes: Large and XLarge shapes follow Atlassian's AWS infrastructure recommendations for Jira Data Center clustered deployments.
The Large tier maps to the Jira 9.12 cost-effective recommendation, while the XLarge tier maps to the Jira 9.12 best-performance recommendation.
disclaimers: Estimate excludes shared-home storage, managed database Multi-AZ overhead, Marketplace app overhead, and other supporting services not explicitly sized in the recommendation summary.
The HA toggle models front-end load balancer overhead only; Atlassian Data Center clustering itself is already represented in the base node counts.

name: Keycloak
description: Self-managed Keycloak for identity, authentication, SSO, and federation across internal and customer-facing apps.

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 25 peak logins per second

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 150 to 500 peak logins per second or heavier federation and brokering

notes: Keycloak node counts follow the official HA guidance, while the backing database is represented as a conservative self-managed planning baseline instead of the managed Aurora examples used in some vendor docs.
CPU-heavy larger tiers reflect the project's guidance that production HA deployments benefit from at least four cores per Keycloak node.
disclaimers: Based on Keycloak's official HA sizing concepts and translated into VM-based planning inputs for self-managed deployments.
Estimate excludes external IdPs, SMTP, user federation backends, separate caching infrastructure, and license costs for any commercial database choice.

name: MinIO
description: Self-managed MinIO object storage for S3-compatible buckets, data lakes, and application blob storage.

ref_arch.version: Community install topologies with current MinIO reference hardware

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 20 TB usable capacity on a single multi-drive node

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 20 to 100 TB usable capacity on a four-node erasure-coded cluster

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 100 TB to 1 PiB usable capacity on an eight-node erasure-coded cluster

notes: Production-oriented MinIO deployments generally start at an erasure-coded multi-node layout, so the HA toggle expands the smallest tier into a four-node cluster.

The published MinIO reference hardware for large-scale object storage is often beefier than the generic VM sizes available here, so these are conservative planning baselines rather than vendor-certified hardware clones.
disclaimers: Based on MinIO community deployment topologies and public reference hardware guidance, translated into VM-based object-storage planning tiers.
Estimate excludes external load balancers beyond the small HA add-on, raw-to-usable erasure coding overhead, backups, replication, and high-bandwidth networking costs.

name: MongoDB Enterprise Advanced
description: Self-managed MongoDB Enterprise Advanced for replicated document databases and sharded operational workloads.

ref_arch.version: MongoDB Manual current self-managed production guidance

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 250 GB working set on a replicated operational cluster

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 250 GB to 1 TB working set on a production replica set

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 1 to 10 TB working set on a sharded cluster

notes: Replica-set and sharded-cluster tiers already include the redundant members needed for production fault tolerance.
Working-set bands are derived from MongoDB's RAM and storage guidance and normalized to the billable VM shapes CloudEstimate supports.
disclaimers: Based on MongoDB self-managed production notes and common MongoDB production topologies, translated into VM-style planning baselines.
Estimate excludes Enterprise Advanced licensing, Ops Manager infrastructure, backups, analytics nodes, and cross-region disaster recovery.

name: Neo4j Enterprise
description: Self-managed Neo4j Enterprise for transactional graph databases, path analysis, and graph-backed applications.

ref_arch.version: Neo4j Operations Manual current clustering guidance

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 100 GB graph store on a three-primary cluster

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 100 GB to 500 GB graph store with read scale-out

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 500 GB to 2 TB graph store or heavier read-dominant graph workloads

notes: Clustered Neo4j Enterprise requires three primaries, so the base tiers already include the core fault-tolerant topology.
Memory-heavy node shapes reflect Neo4j's guidance to balance JVM heap and page cache against the hot graph dataset.
disclaimers: Based on Neo4j Enterprise clustering guidance and translated into VM-based planning tiers for self-managed deployments.
Estimate excludes backups, external load balancing, vector-search accelerators, and any separate analytics clusters.

name: Sonatype Nexus Repository
description: Self-managed Sonatype Nexus Repository for package hosting, caching, and binary distribution across development pipelines.
notes: XS through Large tiers are derived from Sonatype's published cloud-native AWS reference architectures and request-rate profiles.
For clustered tiers, repository node counts follow the reference architecture; the HA toggle adds the published database failover node and a load balancer.
disclaimers: Based on Sonatype Nexus Repository reference architectures and translated into VM-style planning inputs for multi-cloud estimation.
Estimate excludes external object storage capacity, backup infrastructure, and multi-region disaster recovery.

name: Nomad
description: Self-managed Nomad for workload orchestration across VM, container, and batch platforms.

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 50 small jobs on a starter production cluster

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 50 to 300 jobs on a small production scheduler footprint

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 300 to 1,000 jobs or a bootstrap platform for larger fleets

notes: The Nomad server quorum follows HashiCorp production guidance, while the client pools are generic starter capacity and not a substitute for sizing the workloads you run on top.
This entry is most useful as a platform bootstrap estimate because the scheduled jobs themselves usually dominate total cost.
disclaimers: Based on Nomad production guidance and translated into a VM-based control-plane plus starter-worker baseline.
Estimate excludes Consul, Vault, external load balancers, persistent storage for stateful jobs, and the real resource needs of the applications scheduled by Nomad.

name: OpenSearch
description: Self-managed OpenSearch for full-text search, observability pipelines, and analytics clusters.

xs.label: Extra Small
xs.range_description: up to 100 GB hot index data on a single node

m.label: Medium
m.range_description: 100 GB to 1 TB hot data on a cluster with dedicated managers

xl.label: Extra Large
xl.range_description: 1 to 5 TB hot data on a larger scale-out cluster

notes: OpenSearch cluster-manager and data-node separation follows official cluster tuning patterns for production deployments.
NVMe-backed local disks are used for the hot data tier because search and ingest performance tends to be storage-latency sensitive.
disclaimers: Based on OpenSearch cluster tuning and operational guidance, translated into VM-based planning tiers.
Estimate excludes warm or cold tiers, snapshots, machine-learning plugins, remote storage, and network transfer.

name: Redis Enterprise
description: Self-managed Redis Enterprise cluster for low-latency caching, session storage, and data services.
notes: Redis Enterprise clusters need spare capacity to preserve failover headroom.
NVMe-backed storage matters more than CPU once persistence and auto-tiering are enabled.
disclaimers: Based on Redis Enterprise hardware guidance and encoded as a VM-based planning baseline.
Estimate excludes Redis licensing, cross-region replication, backups, and network transfer.
